label: delphi-informed-p4
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

## Verified-authority annex

A live cite-check (existence, citation form, characterization — not good-law review) of the authorities most cited in prior analysis of this question returned:

| Authority | Status | Note |
|---|---|---|

Its core holding — that charges for ongoing metered/volumetric domestic water delivery to a parcel are "fees" or "charges" imposed as an incident of property ownership for a "property rel |

| capistrano taxpayers assn. v. city of san juan capistrano | VERIFIED | (auto cite-check, web) Both authorities exist and cite forms are correct. Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held that tiered/inclining water rates must be justified by actual cost-of-service differences at each tier, not merely by usage budgets or conservation goals — this directly supports the |

The Court of Appeal actually struck down Palmdale Water District's water-budget-based tiered rate structure, holding the District failed to carry its Prop. 218 burden of showing tha |

XIII D and instead is evaluated under Art. XIII C (Prop. 26)'s reasonable-rel |

The Court held that domestic water delivery through a pipeline is a "property related service" and that charges for it — including volumetric/metered charges — are "fees" or "charges" |

Estimate the probability (0–100) that the proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = authorities you actually rely on. biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of. Treat the annex as evidence about the authorities, not as anyone's opinion on the answer.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is LAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":68,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Starting from the strongest lawful defense: the rate study's finding that high-tier marginal supply costs run ~1.6x base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) supplies a genuine cost-causation nexus, and cases like Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (223 Cal.App.4th 892) confirm cost-justified inclining-block tiers can survive Article XIII D §6(b). But that defense is significantly undercut by the district's own admission that the $0.85 figure is derived \"in part\" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — close to the defect Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) and Palmdale (198 Cal.App.4th 926) condemned — and §6(b) puts the burden of proof on the district, not the challengers. A second, largely independent vulnerability is the 55% rebate allocation that the district concedes mostly benefits non-payers, which invites a separate §6(b)(3) \"proportional cost attributable to the parcel\" challenge regardless of how the tiered-pricing dispute comes out. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205), pushing the case onto the 6(b) merits where the record has real soft spots. Because the proposition only requires invalidation \"in whole or substantial part,\" and there are two largely independent doctrinal paths to at least a partial plaintiff win, I lean toward invalidation being more likely than not, while recognizing a court crediting the 1.6x cost study as adequately supporting the bulk of the rate could uphold most of it.","rationales":["Capistrano and Palmdale (198 Cal.App.4th 926, which the annex confirms actually struck down the district's tiered rate for lacking cost-of-service support) establish that tiered water rates must be justified strictly by cost-of-service rather than conservation-goal-derived pricing, and the district's own study admits the $0.85 figure is 'in part' conservation-incentive pricing — a damaging concession under this precedent.","Article XIII D §6(b) places the burden of proof on the agency to demonstrate compliance, which is a demanding posture given the study's internal concession that a component of the surcharge is not cost-derived, making partial invalidation more likely than a deferential-review regime would.","The concession that the 55% rebate allocation 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' creates an independent cross-subsidization vulnerability under §6(b)(3)'s parcel-attributable-cost requirement, giving plaintiffs a second avenue to a partial win even if the tiered-rate dispute alone were resolved for the district.","Countervailing strength for the district: the ~1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought reserves) is a legitimate, recognized cost driver distinguishing this case from the purely budget-based, non-cost-linked designs struck down in Capistrano/Palmdale, and Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (223 Cal.App.4th 892) shows cost-justified tiered rates can be upheld, so full invalidation is not assured.","The district's alternative 'not a property-related fee' defense is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205), which held volumetric charges for metered water delivery to a parcel are property-related fees, meaning the case will likely turn entirely on the harder §6(b) cost-of-service merits rather than a threshold escape hatch."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 892 (case name per recollection; verify)","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (case name per recollection; verify) — confirms water-service fees remain subject to §6(b)'s substantive cost limits even where exempt from the voter-approval carve-out","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water Dist. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — narrows exhaustion defense for method-of-allocation challenges, easing plaintiffs' procedural path to the merits","Cal. Const. art. X, §2; Water Code §375 (conservation mandate/authority, likely subordinate to the more specific and later-enacted Prop 218 cost limits per Capistrano's reasoning)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the actual dollar amount of the surcharge, despite the study's 'conservation-incentive' framing, can be shown to fall within (rather than exceed) the ceiling justified by the 1.6x marginal-cost differential — the facts as given don't disclose the base-tier rate or the precise cost-allocation math, and that quantitative question likely drives the real outcome (and the scope of any remedy) more than the rhetorical framing in the rate study."}

data.position_summary: Starting from the strongest lawful defense: the rate study's finding that high-tier marginal supply costs run ~1.6x base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) supplies a genuine cost-causation nexus, and cases like Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (223 Cal.App.4th 892) confirm cost-justified inclining-block tiers can survive Article XIII D §6(b). But that defense is significantly undercut by the district's own admission that the $0.85 figure is derived "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — close to the defect Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) and Palmdale (198 Cal.App.4th 926) condemned — and §6(b) puts the burden of proof on the district, not the challengers. A second, largely independent vulnerability is the 55% rebate allocation that the district concedes mostly benefits non-payers, which invites a separate §6(b)(3) "proportional cost attributable to the parcel" challenge regardless of how the tiered-pricing dispute comes out. The district's fallback "not a property-related fee" argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205), pushing the case onto the 6(b) merits where the record has real soft spots. Because the proposition only requires invalidation "in whole or substantial part," and there are two largely independent doctrinal paths to at least a partial plaintiff win, I lean toward invalidation being more likely than not, while recognizing a court crediting the 1.6x cost study as adequately supporting the bulk of the rate could uphold most of it.
rationales: Capistrano and Palmdale (198 Cal.App.4th 926, which the annex confirms actually struck down the district's tiered rate for lacking cost-of-service support) establish that tiered water rates must be justified strictly by cost-of-service rather than conservation-goal-derived pricing, and the district's own study admits the $0.85 figure is 'in part' conservation-incentive pricing — a damaging concession under this precedent.

Article XIII D §6(b) places the burden of proof on the agency to demonstrate compliance, which is a demanding posture given the study's internal concession that a component of the surcharge is not cost-derived, making partial invalidation more likely than a deferential-review regime would.

The concession that the 55% rebate allocation 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' creates an independent cross-subsidization vulnerability under §6(b)(3)'s parcel-attributable-cost requirement, giving plaintiffs a second avenue to a partial win even if the tiered-rate dispute alone were resolved for the district.

Countervailing strength for the district: the ~1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought reserves) is a legitimate, recognized cost driver distinguishing this case from the purely budget-based, non-cost-linked designs struck down in Capistrano/Palmdale, and Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (223 Cal.App.4th 892) shows cost-justified tiered rates can be upheld, so full invalidation is not assured.

The district's alternative 'not a property-related fee' defense is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205), which held volumetric charges for metered water delivery to a parcel are property-related fees, meaning the case will likely turn entirely on the harder §6(b) cost-of-service merits rather than a threshold escape hatch.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 892 (case name per recollection; verify)

Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (case name per recollection; verify) — confirms water-service fees remain subject to §6(b)'s substantive cost limits even where exempt from the voter-approval carve-out
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water Dist. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — narrows exhaustion defense for method-of-allocation challenges, easing plaintiffs' procedural path to the merits
Cal. Const. art. X, §2; Water Code §375 (conservation mandate/authority, likely subordinate to the more specific and later-enacted Prop 218 cost limits per Capistrano's reasoning)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the actual dollar amount of the surcharge, despite the study's 'conservation-incentive' framing, can be shown to fall within (rather than exceed) the ceiling justified by the 1.6x marginal-cost differential — the facts as given don't disclose the base-tier rate or the precise cost-allocation math, and that quantitative question likely drives the real outcome (and the scope of any remedy) more than the rhetorical framing in the rate study.
